items.q: Why is gravitational potential energy near a planet treated as negative?
items.correct: Because the zero is defined at infinity; objects bound to the planet have lower (negative) potential energy than at infinity.
items.misconception: Gravitational potential energy is negative because gravity always points downwards.
items.random_wrong: Gravitational potential energy is negative because gravity does negative work.
items.why: Students confuse the negative sign as 'direction' rather than understanding it as a consequence of choosing infinity as the zero point.

items.q: If a car doubles its speed, what happens to its kinetic energy?
items.correct: It quadruples (because KE = 1/2 mv^2 — kinetic energy is proportional to v^2).
items.misconception: Kinetic energy doubles because energy is proportional to speed.
items.random_wrong: Kinetic energy stays the same because mass hasn't changed.
items.why: Students assume linear relationships (double the input, double the output) and forget the v^2 factor in the kinetic energy formula.

items.q: Why does a straw in water look bent?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Light refracts at the surface. “Water magnifies it” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A prism splits white light into.
items.why: It's A spectrum of seven colours. “Only red and blue” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many colours are usually listed in the visible spectrum?
items.why: The real figure is 7. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
